topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed, a grating soundtrack to my rising panic. I stared at the accusation on the screen—a stark block of text outlining the alleged copying. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles ache. Every word they used felt like a deliberate assault, a calculated dismantling of everything I’d poured into that paper. I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles turned white, the cheap laminate digging into my palms. "This is absurd," I managed to hiss, the sound thin and reedy in the cavernous silence of the study hall. I could feel the heat creeping up my neck, a flush that had nothing to do with the room's temperature. I wanted to lash out, to scream the sheer injustice of it all, but I forced my hands to remain still, a desperate attempt to keep the tremor out of my voice.

[story 2]

"Look, I didn't do it," I repeated, my voice flat, devoid of any real inflection. My gaze swept over the professor’s face, cataloging the subtle tightening around his eyes, the slight, condescending tilt of his head. A cold, hard knot formed in my stomach, solidifying into a dense pressure behind my ribs. I shifted in my seat, the cheap plastic chair squeaking in protest. I felt a profound sense of being trapped, as if the air itself had thickened, making each breath a conscious, difficult effort. When the committee started questioning me, I met their eyes, my expression carefully blank, a mask of weary resignation. I kept my hands folded neatly on the table, refusing to offer any gesture that might betray the churning resentment boiling just beneath my skin.

[story 3]

The accusation landed like a physical blow. My vision narrowed, the edges of the room blurring into an irritating smear of beige and grey. I stood up too quickly, the movement jerky, and walked toward the exit, needing distance, needing air that didn't smell of stale coffee and judgment. Each step was weighted, heavy with suppressed fury. When the administrative assistant tried to stop me, I cut her off with a sharp, dismissive wave of my hand. "Don't follow me," I said, my tone clipped and brittle. I could feel the blood pounding in my ears, a frantic drumbeat against the silence. I turned the corner, the sound of my own quickened breathing the only evidence of the storm raging internally. I knew, with a certainty that settled deep in my bones, that this wasn't just about a paper; it was about a complete and utter dismissal of my effort.
